claim: For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 2^n - 2 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 2^(n-1) - 1 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime and n is odd then 2^n - 2 is divisible by 2n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is an odd prime then 2^n ≡ 2 (mod n).
predicate: (not (n > 1)) or (not is_prime(n)) or (n % 2 == 0) or ((2**n) % n == 2 % n)

claim: For all integers n > 1, if n is prime and n ≠ 2 then 2^(n-1) ≡ 1 (mod n).
For all odd primes n, if 2 is a quadratic residue modulo n then 2^((n-1)/2) ≡ 1 (mod n).
For all integers n > 1, if n is an odd prime then n divides 2^n - 2 and also divides 2(2^n - 2).
The prime 2 is the unique even prime, and 2^2 - 2 = 2 is divisible by 2.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then n divides 2(2^(n-1) - 1).
For all odd primes p, 2^((p-1)/2) ≡ ±1 (mod p).
For all odd primes p ≡ 1 (mod 8), 2^((p-1)/2) ≡ 1 (mod p).
For all odd primes p ≡ 7 (mod 8), 2^((p-1)/2) ≡ -1 (mod p).
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime and n is odd then 2^n ≡ 2 (mod 2n).
For all odd primes p, 2^(p-1) - 1 is divisible by p and also by (p-1) when gcd(2, p-1) = 1.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 3^n - 3 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 5^n - 5 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1 and all integers a, if n is prime then a^n - a is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 7^n - 7 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then n^2 - n is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then n divides gcd(2^n - 2, 3^n - 3).
For all odd primes p, the multiplicative order of 2 modulo p divides p - 1.
For all odd primes p such that the order of 2 modulo p equals p - 1, 2 is a primitive root modulo p.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 2^n - 2 ≡ 0 (mod n) and 3^n - 3 ≡ 0 (mod n), hence 2^n + 3^n ≡ 5 (mod n).
For all odd primes p, 2 is a quadratic residue modulo p if and only if p ≡ 1, 7 (mod 8).
For all odd primes p, the Legendre symbol (2/p) = 1 when p ≡ ±1 (mod 8).
For all odd primes p, 2^(p-1) is congruent to 1 modulo p.
For all odd primes p, 2^(2(p-1)) is congruent to 1 modulo p.
For all odd primes n, 2^n - 2 is divisible by 2 and by n, hence by 2n.
For all odd primes n, if n ≡ 1 (mod 4) then 2^n - 2 is divisible by 4n.
For all odd primes n, if n ≡ 3 (mod 4) then 2^n - 2 is divisible by 2n but not always by 4n.
For all primes p > 2, 2^p + 3^p - 5 is divisible by p.
For all odd primes p, p divides 2^p + 3^p - 5 and also p divides 2^p - 2.
For all odd primes p, the exponent of p in 2^(p-1) - 1 is at least 1.
For all odd primes p, vp(2^(p-1) - 1, p) >= 1, and for primes p with order of 2 equal to p-1, vp(2^(p-1) - 1, p) = 1.
For all primes p > 2, the quantity 2^p - 2 has exactly one factor of 2.
For all odd primes p, 2^p - 2 = 2(2^(p-1) - 1) where 2^(p-1) - 1 is odd.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 11^n - 11 is divisible by n.
For all odd primes p, 2^p ≡ 2 (mod p) and p divides 2^p - 2, hence the Chinese Remainder Theorem applies to simultaneous congruences.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or (n % 2 == 0) or ((2**n) % n == 2 % n)

claim: For all odd primes p, p divides lcm(2^p - 2, 3^p - 3) = gcd(2^p - 2, 3^p - 3) * lcm factor.
For all odd primes p where p does not divide 2, the quantity 2^(p-1) - 1 has p as a divisor with multiplicity at least 1.
For all odd primes p ≡ 1 (mod 4) with order of 2 being p-1, the 2-adic valuation of 2^p - 2 equals 1.
For all odd primes p, 2^n ≡ 2 (mod p) holds for n = p, and 2^(n-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) by Fermat's Little Theorem.
For all odd primes p, if 2 is a QR modulo p then 2^((p-1)/2) ≡ 1 (mod p), and conversely.
For all odd primes p > 2, p divides both 2^p - 2 and 2^p + 3^p - 5, so p divides 3^p - 3.
For all odd primes p, the order of 2 modulo p divides φ(p) = p - 1, and if 2 is a primitive root then the order equals p - 1.
For all odd primes n, 2^n - 2 is divisible by both n and 2, and if n ≡ 1 (mod 8) then additional factors of 2 may divide it.
For all odd primes p with primitive root 2, vp(2^(p-1) - 1, p) = 1 and the multiplicative order is exactly p - 1.
For all odd primes p, 2^p - 2 and 3^p - 3 are both divisible by p and by 2, sharing the common factor 2p.
For all odd primes p with 2 being a primitive root, gcd(2^p - 2, φ(p)) is minimized at 2.
For all odd primes p, the sum 2^p - 2 + 3^p - 3 is divisible by both 2 and p, hence by 2p.
